artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

I finally have a quiet half hour while Jacob is at soccer and Mark took Emily to band, so I wanted to write instead of just sending you another quick text.

I’ve been thinking a lot about Dad this week. I pulled out the old notebook where I kept all his endocrinology appointment notes from when he was going back and forth to the University of Missouri Health Care clinic in Columbia. Seeing those A1C numbers slide the wrong way and the dates he skipped follow‑ups because he didn’t have the gas money for the 120‑mile round trip brought it all back. I used those same notes back in January 2022 when I was sketching out the screening questions and follow‑up scripts for our Riverbend Vision & Wellness days, trying to catch people before they end up where he did.

It hit me again how many of his appointments at St. Luke’s in Kansas City and then in 2019 at St. Mary’s in Jefferson City got pushed or canceled over $40 of gas or an $85 copay. That’s exactly the kind of thing we’re seeing now at the clinics. On Saturday at the food pantry, an older gentleman told me he’d been rationing his metformin and skipping insulin doses since December because of a $620 bill at Riverbend Regional. We sat in the pantry’s back office for 45 minutes on the phone with billing and got them to set up a $25‑a‑month plan so he could refill his meds. I kept thinking, “I wish someone had done this with Dad.”

Day‑to‑day life is the usual chaos—Emily’s Leo Club projects, Jacob’s science fair, Mark grading AP essays at the kitchen table—but that through‑line of Dad’s story is always there under it all.

Love you,
Patricia

metadata.subject: Thinking about Dad and the clinics

artifacts.content:
Personal planning / brain dump – late night at the dining table

I keep circling back to Caleb from Lincoln Elementary. Back in October 2023, when we did that Mobile Vision & Wellness day at the school, his screening showed both severe myopia and a scary high blood sugar. I remember sitting in my office at Riverbend Community Health Partnership on October 17, calling his mom, walking her through the county charity‑care forms, and then driving with them to Children’s Mercy in Kansas City for that first pediatric endocrinology visit. Hearing the doctor say he was already in early‑stage type 2 diabetes at age nine was like watching Dad’s story replay in fast‑forward.

I want to use Caleb’s case, plus Jasmine at Eastside and Mr. Collins from the pantry, when we train new volunteers in January 2024—real stories that show why the integrated model matters. But I also need to be careful about details and privacy. Same issue with Maria from St. Mark’s whose retinal exam showed advanced diabetic retinopathy and who now has to go back and forth to St. Joseph’s in Kansas City for laser treatments over the next 6–8 months. We’re quietly lining up rides through the Lions, the church, and her sister.

Note to self: our diabetes/vision project honestly isn’t that “innovative” in terms of activities. We’re doing the same basic screenings and health‑fair type booths everyone does; what’s different is the persistence and the follow‑through.

Also: remember to check in with Emily about how she’s feeling using Caleb’s story in training, since he’s her neighbor and she’s been helping translate for his mom.

metadata.title: Clinic stories to use (carefully) in training

artifacts.content:
Hi Linda,

I wanted to jot down some thoughts while Saturday is still fresh in my mind.

The March 9 clinic at the Riverbend Food Pantry went well on paper—63 people screened in about four hours—but I keep replaying my conversation with Mr. Alvarez. When he sat down with me in the little back office and admitted he’d been rationing his metformin and skipping insulin since December 2023 because of that $620 bill at Riverbend Regional, my stomach dropped. Sitting on hold with the hospital billing department together and finally getting them to agree to a $25/month plan felt like a small miracle, but also like something volunteers shouldn’t have to improvise.

It reminded me of the Oak Terrace situation last year. Linda Chavez pulled me aside in May 2023 to tell me that Mr. Jenkins—the 63‑year‑old we’d screened in February—had been evicted in April after his vision deteriorated, he lost his deli job at Riverbend Fresh Market, and he fell behind on rent. You and I talked later at Healthy Riverbend about how we only heard about it after the eviction notice. We need a better way to flag people who are clearly sliding toward crisis.

I also keep thinking about Mom sitting in the waiting area at the February pantry clinic, quietly watching those 63 people cycle through, then admitting on the drive back to Brookfield that she skipped her own eye exam when her Medicare Part D premium jumped $47/month and the furnace died.

On the bright side, the volunteer team is getting smoother with setup and teardown, and the pantry staff were wonderful as always. But days like this make the work feel both crucial and impossibly small at the same time.

See you Thursday,
Patricia

metadata.subject: Reflections after pantry clinic + Oak Terrace

artifacts.content:
Debrief – project design + data (written at home after kids’ bedtime)

I spent this afternoon pulling de‑identified numbers from MyLion and then cross‑checking them against the password‑protected spreadsheet on my home laptop—the one with the 287 names, phone numbers, and high‑risk flags for people we saw between July 2022 and June 2023. It dawned on me (again) that I’ve basically become the unofficial keeper of a quasi‑medical registry for Riverbend without any kind of formal HIPAA‑style agreement. I already emailed Greg to ask about better encryption and whether we should move the file off my personal machine.

While I was at it, I went back through the notes from May 3, 2023 with Lisa Turner at Riverbend Middle. That’s when she showed me her confidential list of 27 kids who failed vision screenings and never got follow‑up, including the family with the $326 balance at Riverbend Eye Care Associates. That conversation is what pushed me to start quarterly school‑based clinics in August 2023.

On the systems side, I keep meaning to use more of the official Lions resources—MyLion webinars, GST trainings, the global cause guides—but in reality I’ve mostly leaned on my public health background and just figured things out as we went. I never did sign up for those online reporting sessions; there’s always one more email from the county or a kid who needs a ride.

Action items: talk with Sarah (district GST) again about what absolutely must be in MyLion vs. what stays in our own files, and start drafting a simple data‑sharing and privacy protocol for the club.

Hi Greg,

Thanks again for always being my unofficial tech support.

I had a bit of a wake‑up call during our internal review last week. To make sense of our follow‑up rates, I exported the de‑identified counts from MyLion and then opened the master spreadsheet I keep on my personal laptop here at home. That file has 287 individual records from July 2022 through June 2023—names, phone numbers, screening results, and little flags for “possible diabetes,” “urgent vision referral,” etc. Between that and the notes I was tempted to put into MyLion (like the 62‑year‑old neighbor from Willow Bend whose blood sugar was over 300 at the February 17, 2024 St. Mark’s clinic), I realized I’m skating way too close to something that looks like a medical registry.

After my Zoom with Lion Sarah Mitchell on March 4, I went back into MyLion and scrubbed the identifiable details, but I’m still uneasy about the spreadsheet living on my personal laptop, even with a password. Can you help me think through encryption or a better storage option? Also, longer term, we probably need a simple data/privacy policy for the club so this isn’t just “whatever Patricia thinks is reasonable.”

Separate but related: when I look back, we honestly didn’t use much from LCI beyond downloading the logo that first year. I was so overwhelmed just getting the mobile days off the ground that I never had the bandwidth to dig into the toolkits or online trainings. If you’ve found any tech‑related LCI resources that are actually practical, I’m all ears.

Appreciate you,
Patricia

metadata.subject: Clinic data, privacy, and tech help

artifacts.content:
Late‑night reflection – why this still feels so hard sometimes

Some days I feel like the Riverbend Vision & Wellness project is the most meaningful thing I’ve ever helped build. Other days, it feels like we’re pushing a boulder up a hill that nobody asked us to climb.

When we first floated the idea, a lot of people in town shrugged. More than once I’ve heard some version of, “Sure, screenings are nice, but we’ve got bigger problems.” And they’re not wrong—families are juggling two or three jobs, caregiving, housing insecurity. What we’re doing is a tiny drop in a very leaky bucket.

Partnerships have been harder than I expected, too. We’ve had wonderful allies—Riverbend Vision Associates, the food pantry, St. Mark’s—but a couple of organizations turned us down flat early on because they couldn’t see what was in it for them. And the neighboring town’s Lions club, the one we tried to rope in that first year, bailed after we couldn’t agree on whose logo went where and who would get “credit.” It was so disheartening that we finally just moved ahead on our own.

Meanwhile, inside our own club it’s the same small core of us hauling tables and running registration while a few officers drift in just in time for the photo op. I know everyone is busy, but on nights like this, after the kids are in bed and my feet are still throbbing from a Saturday clinic, I catch myself wondering how long we can sustain this pace.

And yet—then I picture Caleb at Children’s Mercy, or Maria heading up to St. Joseph’s for her laser treatment, or Mom quietly watching 63 neighbors get screened at the pantry. That’s usually enough to get me to open the laptop and start planning the next one.

metadata.title: Why this feels both essential and small

artifacts.content:
Hi Sara,

Catching up between meetings and wanted to get some thoughts to you while they’re fresh.

I finally completed the LCI Learn “Serving Safely” module on January 9 from my office at Riverbend Community Health Partnership. I paid extra attention to the confidentiality pieces because of that November 2023 scare at Riverbend Middle, when a volunteer left the paper log with A1C results sitting on a table in the gym. We got a call that sounded suspiciously like the beginning of a HIPAA complaint. For the January 27 clinic back at the middle school, I built a mandatory 30‑minute privacy briefing for everyone using some of the module’s language.

It’s funny, because in other ways we’ve leaned way more on my public health training than on official Lions materials. We modeled most of the project after a template I found on the LCI site, so the structure isn’t terribly original, but I didn’t really dig into the global cause guides the way I probably should have.

On the home front, things are their usual juggling act. Emily is thriving in the Leo Club but still gets anxious around big presentations; I’m proud she stood up with me at the Riverbend City Council meeting on August 14, 2023 to talk about the clinics. Jacob is counting down to soccer season. Mom has another eye check in Brookfield next month, and I’ll tack it onto a grocery run like always.

If you have any sample privacy scripts or checklists you like for volunteer‑run screenings, I’d love to see them.

Best,
Patricia

metadata.subject: Confidentiality, clinics, and a bit of life

artifacts.content:
End‑of‑year vent / reflection – not for sharing

Another long clinic season wrapped, and I’m exhausted in that bone‑deep way that doesn’t go away after one quiet weekend.

I keep thinking about March 2023, when I nearly passed out at the Riverbend Food Share clinic and wound up at Riverbend General Hospital overnight. Dr. Lawson adjusted my meds and told me point‑blank I couldn’t keep doing 50–60 volunteer hours a month on top of my full‑time job, at least not if I wanted to be around for Emily and Jacob. I’ve tried to stick closer to 25–30 hours since, but it still adds up.

Some of the discouragement is internal. Our general membership participation has been so hit‑or‑miss. It’s the same half‑dozen of us who show up early, stay late, and handle the messy parts, while a few officers seem to materialize only when there’s a newspaper photographer or a ribbon to cut. I don’t begrudge anyone a lighter season, but it’s hard not to feel resentful when my Saturdays disappear and I’m the one answering late‑night calls about a neighbor’s blood sugar.

And then there’s the reporting mess. We haven’t submitted half of what we’ve actually done into MyLion over the past few years. Between my own resistance to more screen time and the clunky interface, I’ve let a lot of events slide. On paper it probably looks like our club is barely doing anything, when in reality we’re running ourselves ragged.

Still, when I flip through my little story notebook—Caleb, Mr. Alvarez, Maria, Mom at the pantry—I remember why we started. Maybe next year the balance will feel a little saner.

From: Patricia Reynolds <patricia.reynolds@riverbendlions.org>
To: Self – grant writing folder (Draft narrative)
Date: 2024-01-22
Subject: DRAFT – Personal motivation & partnerships (do not send)

[Grant narrative scratchpad – for my eyes only]

When I talk about why the Riverbend Vision & Wellness Outreach Initiative matters, I always come back to my dad, Thomas Hayes. Between January–August 2021 his vision declined so fast, and by the time he died from diabetes complications at Mercy Hospital in Brookfield, MO, I had a notebook full of the $85 copay eye appointments he canceled in Brookfield because he couldn’t get a ride or afford it. I still remember sitting with him at the St. Mary’s Hospital ophthalmology clinic in Jefferson City in June 2019, realizing how many chances we’d missed.

That’s why, when we launched monthly clinics on the east side of Riverbend in March 2022, I pushed hard for free diabetic retinopathy screenings and transportation vouchers for older adults. It’s also what led me, in March 2022, to meet privately with his endocrinologist, Dr. Karen Liu, at the River County Health Department low‑cost clinic. I asked if she’d advise our Lions project; now she comes to our quarterly coordination meetings at the Riverbend Community Health Partnership office and shares de‑identified trends so we don’t repeat Dad’s story.

On the youth side, Emily’s experience in September 2022 at Riverbend High School was the turning point. She failed her school vision screening, and at Dr. Carter’s office at Riverbend Vision Center we learned her headaches and slipping Algebra II grades were tied to significant astigmatism. After paying the $35 co‑pay for Emily’s exam, I vowed that every Mobile Vision & Wellness Day would include a pathway to same‑week optometry appointments. We piloted that referral system with Riverbend Vision Center in November 2022.

[Note to self: careful not to over‑share Dad’s medical details in the final version.]

metadata.subject: DRAFT – Personal motivation & partnerships (do not send)

artifacts.content:
From: Patricia Reynolds <patricia.reynolds@riverbendlions.org>
To: Michael Greene <mgreene@healthyriverbend.org>
Cc: (draft – not yet sent)
Date: 2024-01-19
Subject: CONFIDENTIAL – coalition history + family intersections (notes)

Michael,

Drafting this for myself before I decide how much to actually share.

When you referenced the HRSA history at the Healthy Riverbend Coalition strategy meeting at the Riverbend Community Center on 1/18/24, it brought a lot back. In 2021 the HRSA program officer nearly pulled our three‑year grant for Riverbend Community Health Partnership over the perceived conflict between my grant‑funded coordinator role and my Lions Club Service Chair position. We ended up submitting a formal conflict management plan in March 2021 explaining how I separate my paid duties from the Lions clinics. That’s part of why the coalition now lists the Lions project as an external community partner instead of an internal program.

This work is tangled up with our family life too. In October 2022 my mom, Carolyn Hayes, had a minor car accident on Route 36 outside Brookfield after misjudging distance at night. Her ophthalmologist at Brookfield Eye Center later tied it to worsening diabetic retinopathy. Since then, I’ve been driving her the 68‑mile round trip to our Riverbend Vision & Wellness Outreach clinics at the Riverbend Community Center every other month for follow‑up screenings and transportation coordination, sometimes using my RCHP mileage reimbursement when Mark has late nights at Riverbend High School.

The only time we’ve ever canceled a clinic was May 6, 2023 at St. Mark’s Church in Riverbend, MO. I had to be at the Jackson County Courthouse in Kansas City that day over a contested bill from my father Thomas Hayes’s final three days in the ICU at Riverbend General Hospital, where he died from diabetes‑related complications. Because no one else was fully trained on the equipment and consent forms, we had to postpone. Hard lesson: we need at least two backup coordinators for every event.

Patricia

(Draft – not yet sent)

Google Doc – Personal reflection & story bank (Patricia M. Reynolds)
Last edited: 2024-07-03

Section: Youth & family stories I might reference (CONFIDENTIAL – do not share names publicly)

1) Emily’s screenings + Leo Club

Because Emily Reynolds is a junior at Riverbend High School and active in the Leo Club, a lot of this work feels close to home. In September 2023, her counselor, Ms. Rachel Ortiz, called about Emily’s anxiety after my mom, Carolyn Hayes, had a minor stroke and new vision changes. We met in Ms. Ortiz’s office at Riverbend High School to talk it through; that’s when I mentioned our Lions vision and glucose screening project. She suggested looping in the Riverbend High School Leo Club and shared (with Mom’s permission) that several students are caring for grandparents with diabetes complications, so we tailored the Leo partnership toward youth caregivers.

Emily’s own health has been a quiet driver too. In Spring 2023 she struggled with disordered eating and rapid weight loss. When I made an LCI‑branded flyer at home on Willow Bend Drive on 10/10/23, using the official LCI logo files and branding guidelines, I originally wrote that the project was inspired by her struggles. Mark Reynolds and I decided that was too personal to print on something we’d hand out at Riverbend High School and Riverbend Middle School, so I deleted that line before we printed 250 copies at the Riverbend Community Health Partnership office.

2) Classmate’s undocumented family

In October 2023, during a Leo Club meeting at Riverbend High, one of Emily’s 10th‑grade classmates told me her undocumented parents were avoiding eye care because they were afraid Riverbend General Hospital would ask for Social Security numbers and report them. At our November 4, 2023 pop‑up in the Riverbend Housing Authority community room, we screened both parents and found her father’s blood pressure was dangerously high. We linked him to the local free clinic’s sliding‑scale program and helped them complete intake without insurance or SSNs. Emily later said her friend told her, “Our family might actually be able to stay here and be okay.”

3) LCI tools + our own family

We leaned heavily on the LCI Diabetes Awareness and Action Toolkit after my dad, Thomas Hayes, lost most of his vision and died at St. Luke’s Hospital in Kansas City, MO in March 2021. I used the toolkit’s screening questions when I sat with Mom for her diabetes risk checklist at her primary care visit on April 12, 2023, then copied that checklist format into intake forms for our April–June 2024 Riverbend Vision & Wellness clinics at St. Mark’s Church.

4) Budget + Mark’s stress leave

For budgeting, I downloaded the LCIF grant application templates in August 2023 and used their sample budget lines almost verbatim for the $7,500 funding request we submitted to the Riverbend Community Hospital Foundation on September 5, 2023. In that proposal I noted that Mark had taken unpaid leave from Riverbend High School in May 2023 for stress‑related health issues, which meant we couldn’t personally float equipment costs and were relying more heavily on the LCIF‑style budget justification.
